title: Macramé curtain tieback
subtitle: A pair of knotted tiebacks at 50 cm finished length in 4 mm natural cotton cord
excerpt: Each tieback is made from six cords of 4 mm cotton macramé cord at 200 cm each, mounted with lark's head knots on a wooden bead and knotted in an alternating-square body. A figure-8 knot at each end keeps the bead in place and the finished length is 50 cm. Made in pairs, these replace standard fabric tiebacks on linen, cotton, or voile curtains.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. The alternating-square knot structure is the canonical mid-century macramé body, documented throughout the macramé revival of the 1960s and 1970s. The figure-8 bead-stop is a standard sailors' ropework stopper knot, referenced in Graumont and Hensel, Encyclopedia of Knots and Fancy Rope Work (1939), Cornell Maritime Press.

glossaryTerms.definition: A length of cord, fabric, or knotted material looped around a curtain panel to hold it back from the window and off the floor. Tiebacks attach to a hook or cleat fixed to the wall beside the window frame. The finished length of the tieback determines how far the curtain is drawn back and how deeply it drapes.

In macramé, the cord that actively forms the knot by moving over, under, or around the other cords. In a square knot the two outer cords are the working cords and the two inner cords are the filler cords, which remain stationary. Working cords are consumed faster than filler cords and should be cut longer when the pattern calls for unequal cord lengths.

content.text: Each
tieback

in this pair is made from six cords of 4 mm natural cotton macramé cord, each cut at 200 cm. All six are mounted with lark's head knots on a 30 to 35 mm wooden bead threaded onto the first cord before knotting, giving twelve
working cords

at 100 cm each after mounting. The body is knotted in alternating-square knots to a finished length of 50 cm, then a figure-8 stopper knot at each end keeps the wooden bead in place. The tiebacks loop around the curtain and hook to a wall cleat. You need 1200 cm of cord per tieback, 2400 cm for the pair.
What you need

items.name: Wooden beads, 30 to 35 mm bore
items.qty: 4 (2 per tieback, one at each end)

items.name: Blunt needle (size 18 tapestry), optional for weaving in ends

content.text: The knots
Three knot types build each tieback: the lark's head to mount cords on the bead mounting cord, alternating-square knots for the body, and the figure-8 stopper at the closing bead.

Lark's head: fold a cord in half, pass the loop over the mounting cord (or around the wooden bead's bore), and pull both tails through the loop from front to back. Pull firmly. After six cords are mounted this way, twelve working ends hang ready for knotting.

definition.caption: Lark's head: fold cord in half, pass loop over mounting cord, pull both tails through.

content.text: Square knot: using four cords (two working, two filler), take the left working cord over the two filler cords and under the right working cord. Take the right working cord under the filler cords and up through the loop formed by the left cord. Pull both working cords outward to close the first half-knot. Reverse the movement to complete the square: left cord now goes under the fillers, right cord over the fillers and up through the new loop. Pull firmly to seat the knot.

definition.caption: Square knot: two working cords around two filler cords, tied in two half-knot moves.

content.text: Alternating-square knot: the first row ties square knots in groups of four cords across the full width. The second row drops the outer two cords of each group and picks up two cords from the adjacent group, offsetting the knot so it falls between the knots above. Alternating every row builds the lattice body of the tieback.

definition.caption: Alternating-square knot: offset row by one cord pair to build a diagonal lattice.

content.text: Figure-8 knot: make a loop in the cord, bring the end over the standing part, behind, and up through the loop from below. The resulting knot sits as a firm figure-of-eight stopper that will not pass through a bead's bore. Use it at both ends of the tieback, tying it against each wooden bead so the bead cannot slip off the cord.

definition.caption: Figure-8 stopper: cord makes a figure-of-eight before the bead to prevent it sliding off.

content.text: Method
Step 1: Cut the cords to length

Cut twelve cords at 200 cm each for the first tieback. Use the cord-measuring tape to mark each cut point accurately. Lay the cords flat, stack them in pairs to check the lengths match, and trim any that are out by more than 1 cm. Repeat for the second tieback. You will have 24 cords at 200 cm total.
Step 2: Thread the first bead and mount the cords

Take one cord and thread it through the bore of a 30 to 35 mm wooden bead, pulling until the bead sits at the centre of the cord. This cord becomes the mounting cord for the lark's head mounts. Pin the mounting cord horizontally to the macramé board so the bead is held steady with equal lengths of mounting cord on each side.

Mount the remaining eleven cords to the mounting cord using lark's head knots, distributing them evenly on both sides of the bead: five cords to the left and five to the right of the bead, each mounted directly onto the mounting cord. Pull each lark's head firmly. After mounting, fourteen working ends hang from the mounting cord (twelve from the mounted cords plus the two tails of the mounting cord itself, which also become working cords). Slide all cords together snugly beside the bead before beginning the body.
Step 3: Knot the alternating-square body

Divide the working ends into three groups of four cords (with the two remaining mounting-cord tails assigned to the outer groups to make them groups of four). You now have three groups: left four, middle four, right four. Tie a square knot in each group leaving about 5 mm of space above each knot, for a total of three knots in the first row.

For the second row, drop the outer two cords of each outer group and the outer two cords of the middle group, then tie one square knot using cords 3, 4, 5, 6 and one using cords 7, 8, 9, 10, leaving the same 5 mm spacing above. Continue alternating rows in this way until the knotted body measures 46 to 47 cm, which will bring the tieback to its finished length of 50 cm once the closing bead and stopper knots are added. Keep the spacing between knots consistent by pinning the work flat to the board and measuring at intervals.
Step 4: Thread and fix the closing bead

Gather all working ends together and thread them through the bore of the second wooden bead. Pull the bead snug against the last row of knots. Below the bead, tie a figure-8 stopper knot in each working end individually, or knot two or three cords together if the bead bore is too small to accommodate individual stoppers alongside each other. Pull each stopper knot tight against the base of the bead so it cannot slide back through the bore. Trim the ends to 3 to 4 cm below the stopper knots for a short fringe, or trim flush if no fringe is wanted.
Step 5: Finish the opening end

Return to the opening end at the first bead. The two tails of the mounting cord extend beyond the bead. Tie a figure-8 stopper knot in each of the two cord tails, pulling them snug against the bead so the bead cannot slide outward. Trim the two cord tails to match the fringe length on the closing end. If any ends look untidy, thread them back into the body using a blunt needle and trim flush. Make the second tieback in the same way and hang both to check they are the same length before installing.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The knotted body twists instead of lying flat
items.cause: The alternating-square rows were pulled more firmly on one side, or the work was not pinned flat between rows
items.fix: Pin the work to the macramé board at both selvedge edges after every two rows and check that the cords run parallel. If one side has drawn in, ease the cords outward before tying the next row. A board with a grid printed on it makes it easier to see when the work is pulling to one side.

items.problem: The wooden bead does not sit at the end of the tieback; the cord body starts too far from the bead
items.cause: The lark's head cords were not pulled firmly against the bead before knotting began, leaving a gap of bare cord
items.fix: Slide all the lark's head knots up to the bead before tying the first square knot row. Pin the bead in place on the board so it cannot move while you tension the first few rows.

items.problem: The tieback is the right total length but the knotted section is too short, leaving long bare tails
items.cause: The figure-8 stoppers and bead took more length than expected
items.fix: Measure at the 40 cm mark while knotting and check the remaining working-cord length. If less than 15 cm remains on the working cords, stop knotting one or two rows earlier and let the closing bead and stoppers take up the remaining length. Plan this adjustment before the last few rows rather than after.
